topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The plastic stick lay on the worn wooden table, the results glowing a stark, undeniable truth. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged thing in my chest. I stared at the screen, the unfamiliar names swimming before my eyes, each one a phantom limb I never knew I was missing. A tremor ran through my hands, causing the lukewarm coffee cup to rattle precariously. It felt like the foundation of my entire life had just crumbled into dust. I pushed the chair back, the scrape of wood against the floor echoing loudly in the sudden silence of the room. I didn't move toward the door; instead, I sank onto the floor, curling into a tight, small ball, the weight of the revelation pressing down until I could barely draw a full breath.

[story 2]

When the technician finally spoke, the words hung in the sterile air like shards of glass. "The match is confirmed." I kept my gaze fixed on the beige wall, refusing to meet anyone's eyes. My throat tightened, a thick, suffocating band around my windpipe. I wanted to argue, to demand clarification, but the sound caught in my throat, emerging only as a weak, choked whisper. I slowly reached out and touched my own cheek, the skin feeling alien, like it belonged to someone else entirely. A single, hot tear escaped, tracing a slow, silent path down my jawline. I scrubbed at it quickly with the heel of my hand, trying to erase the evidence of the sudden, profound disorientation washing over me.

[story 3]

I reread the report three times, the ink blurring under my anxious gaze. It wasn't a shock that paralyzed me; it was a deep, hollow ache, a vast emptiness opening up in my core. My shoulders slumped, the tension that had been coiled tight for months finally dissolving into a heavy, leaden droop. I picked up a stray photograph—a blurry image of a family I’d never known—and let it fall back onto the desk. The sound was soft, insignificant, yet it felt monumental. I closed my eyes, focusing intensely on the dull ache behind my sternum, trying to anchor myself against the dizzying sense of displacement. Every muscle in my body felt heavy, weighted down by an inexplicable sense of loss.
